[157:47]I do support cutting the budget.

[157:50]Okay.

[157:51]And I've said this for a very, very long time.

[157:53]I've been doing the show for eight years.

[158:00]Because there are a lot of people that subscribe to modern monetary theory.

[158:04]There's a lot of people that think the debt is like theoretical.

[158:08]And lately, we're seeing that as not true.

[158:11]Because now that we have this persistent inflation, and the inflation is unanchored from the Federal

[158:24]Now that we have interest rates that are above zero

[158:27]for really like the first time in 20 years,

[158:31]the chickens are coming home to roost.

[158:33]The debt has been accumulating for all this time.

[158:36]Trillions are being added to the debt every year.

[158:39]The debt is now north of $30 trillion.

[158:42]And again, for 20 years,

[158:43]that didn't matter because interest rates were zero

[158:45]since the housing bust in 2006.

[158:49]Now that interest rates are coming up

[158:51]because inflation's coming up,

[158:53]Now, we're paying a ton of interest on that debt.

[158:57]And if the interest remains high to combat inflation and the deficits keep going up or

[159:04]they stay high and the debt keeps growing, the interest keeps climbing along with the

[159:09]other liabilities and you get into a situation where the government is functionally bankrupt.

[159:15]It's a big problem and I've been saying it for a long time.

[159:21]what Doge is doing. In principle, when Doge goes into the federal departments and agencies

[159:28]and they're looking for waste, fraud, and abuse to cut, and they're firing personnel,

[159:36]I support this. And by the way, I support it for multiple reasons. I support it for

[159:41]fiscal reasons. I think that is responsible, which is to say it is making the country solvent

[159:52]But I also support it for political reasons because at the same time, and this is another way to think about it

[159:57]Maybe this is the real reason

[159:59]When you retire, this is what they say when you retire all government employees, that's the

[160:07]Neoreactionary terminology for this, not only are you saving money and there's a cost savings,

[160:14]but you're also removing all of these personnel who have a distinct interest.

[160:20]And I said this last week, the bureaucracy in Washington, DC, the permanent civil service

[160:30]They do not get replaced from one administration to the next.

[160:34]So you have people in the Department of Education, in the USAID, the Foreign Service, the State Department,

[160:40]Department of Energy, they've been working these government jobs for decades.

[160:46]And as such, as government workers, they've become a unique class, distinct from the private sector,

[160:53]even distinct from what we conventionally think of as the political system.

[160:59]These are people that their politics is bound up

[161:04]with their interest as a class.

[161:06]They will never vote for shrinking government

[161:09]because that means losing their job.

[161:11]Their class interest is a political interest.

[161:14]And so they start to develop

[161:15]all these other political ideas.

[161:17]Like for example, the State Department favors

[161:21]a massive footprint of the United States

[161:23]all around the world because that means

[161:25]that they're in business.

[161:28]Their economic class interest is their political interest.

[161:32]Now, this is a problem because they control the political system.

[161:36]So, you have this political class running the political system and their politics is protecting the political system.

[161:46]So, the purpose of the system is no longer to, let's say, for example...

[161:53]educate Americans or have cheap energy or influence the other countries in America's

[162:02]interest.

[162:03]The purpose of the system becomes to perpetuate the system itself, like a teacher's union.

[162:11]You know, it started out as this is how we're going to get teachers paid, then it turns

[162:16]into, well, you know, we need more money so that we can make more money, so it becomes

[162:21]a self-perpetuating system.

[162:23]So for economic, fiscal, and political reasons, I support what they're doing.

[162:30]But here are my criticisms of it, and I've said this at the same time.

[162:40]Because, and everybody knows this, anybody that just looks at the numbers, the federal

[162:47]expenditures are almost all, 90% of it is two things, entitlements and defense.

[162:55]That's it.

[162:57]It's these liabilities, social security, Medicare, Medicaid, those are the big ones.

[163:06]$5 trillion in 2024 out of $6.75 trillion was those entitlements. And those are protected

[163:14]by law. Congress can't come in and cut those year over year like they could when they're

[163:21]appropriating money for the federal departments and agencies. That's considered mandatory

[163:31]as opposed to discretionary spending,

[163:34]or does that Congress's discretion,

[163:36]whether they will appropriate or not,

[163:39]whether they will fund or whether they will cut.

[163:41]So the vast majority of it,

[163:44]the majority of it is entitlements,

[163:46]mandatory spending that they can't touch.

[163:49]And then within discretionary spending,

[163:51]the stuff that they can touch,

[163:54]the majority of that,

[163:55]is defense. It's a military. And that goes up every single year. It goes up under Republicans.

[164:01]It goes up under Democrats. It always goes up. It's going to be something like $900 billion.

[164:08]Maybe a trillion dollars this year up from $800 billion when Biden was elected. So defense,

[164:21]What's left is what you would call,

[164:24]what you would categorize as non-defense

[164:28]discretionary spending.

[164:29]That's the departments, that's the agencies,

[164:32]that's everything else.

[164:33]That's 10% of federal budgetary outlays.

[164:38]That's 10% of what's going out,

[164:42]what's going out the door in spending.

[164:44]And that is what they're cutting.

[164:47]That is what they're going in and gutting and they want to close down the Department of Education and they're gutting the Department of Energy

[164:53]and they're gutting the State Department and they're gutting and

[164:57]It's nice and it's good like I said for the aforementioned reasons. I support it more for the political reasons than anything in this case

[165:06]But in terms of the cost savings

[165:09]How substantial will that really be in the long term?

[165:14]I don't think it's that significant.

[165:16]It's especially not significant when you consider

[165:19]that the source of future spending, future liabilities,

[165:25]it's all coming from entitlements,

[165:27]interest on the debt and defense.

[165:31]So presently those are the big expenditures

[165:34]in the future, those are the big expenditures.

[165:38]And the big problem that nobody wants to talk about is in particular, the unfunded liabilities,

[165:43]which are all of these retirees, all these people that will retire at the age of 65,

[165:50]they're going to live to be 100 or 90 or whatever, especially with life extension.

[166:03]there's gonna be more old people than young people. The old people are gonna

[166:07]cost more to take care of and retirement and health care benefits than young

[166:12]people are putting into the pot. That has always been the fiscal crisis. That has

[166:17]always been the debt crisis. Ever since the New Deal and the Great Society, that

[166:23]was always the math problem that they had.

[166:26]And yet Doge is coming in and saying, well, we're going to cut the department of education.

[166:31]Look, we saved a few billion dollars. We're talking $100 trillion in unfunded liabilities.

[166:37]So this Nikki Nack, like, well, we're not sending 20 million in condoms to Tanzania.

[166:43]I mean, okay, that, that's great, but it's not really doing much. So that's one. Here's

[166:50]the other problem with this. There's a few problems. Here's my other issue.

[166:56]And I, we talked a little bit about this last night, so I don't want to spend too much time

[167:01]on it tonight.

[167:02]But when I see Trump out there reading out the list of wasteful government programs and

[167:23]twice, reputation destroyed in the media. All his supporters, some of them jailed, doxxed,

[167:42]that what this is is like a 90s era liberalism.

[167:47]He said, what we're doing is what the Democrats talked about

[167:58]for fiscal conservatism, for doge to go in and fight

[168:04]waste, fraud, and abuse.

[169:12]And people say, well, you know,

[169:16]well, it's good that they're doing it.

[169:17]And I agree.

[169:18]Like I said, I agree.

[169:19]But where are the priorities?

[169:22]If the priority is cutting the spending,

[169:24]it should be defense and entitlements.

[169:26]But I don't even think cutting the spending

[169:27]should be the priority.

[169:37]one illegal immigrant costs the government

[169:41]in benefits and in-kind transfers.

[169:48]on an individual illegal immigrant over their lifetime,

[169:51]it's like $127,000 for every single one.

[170:01]came in in four years under Biden 10 million times a hundred and 27,000

[170:11]That's what that's trillions of dollars

[170:14]For those people that's like what one two trillion dollars or something like this

[170:19]And that's just the amount of people that came in under Biden

[170:21]That's not counting the children that they'll have who will

[170:30]That's not counting people from previous administrations that came in. If we have an illegal immigrant population of 40 or 50 million people,

[170:38]that's a considerable amount of money.

[170:41]And that's not taking into account all kinds of other economic costs that are involved.

[170:46]So if the priority were deporting illegal immigrants,

[170:50]Not only do you solve a major problem that is not economic in nature,

[170:55]but the knock-on effect, the second order effect, is that you actually save money in the long run.

[171:03]How much did the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan cost over their life?

[171:08]Global war on terror, all these authorization of use of military force at something like $8 trillion.

[171:17]Iraq, Afghanistan, Syria, Yemen, Libya, all these countries. And that's another thing where

[171:24]again, it's not even strictly economic in nature. And yet the second order of fact is

[171:34]the Kennedy center and the JFK library and stuff like that? Or should the focus be on

[171:52]I assume there's a negotiation

[171:56]But I want to see tariff man, I want to see industrial policy

[172:15]for any kind of other actions that would foster industry in America. I see corporate tax breaks.

[172:26]is, you know, it's not something I'm necessarily against in itself, but we need a very targeted,

[172:34]directed industrial policy that is meant to protect the advanced industries of the future.

[172:44]We need subsidies.

[172:45]We need government research and development.

[187:28]with another country, that's actually

[187:29]when you need a diplomatic relationship,

[187:32]because that's how you negotiate an end to the war.

[187:37]That's also, by the way, how you negotiate other things.

[187:43]That's how you coordinate in this very peculiar conflict,

[187:47]the rules of engagement.

[188:36]This is what leads to a world war.

[188:37]This is what leads to a nuclear war,

[188:39]especially when you have two nuclear superpowers

[188:41]which are engaged in a proxy war,

[188:44]which is increasingly a very direct.

[192:19]It says, quote, following a high-level meeting between US and Russian officials, the state

[192:32]US-Russian diplomacy sank during the first Trump administration, and Joe Biden cut nearly

[192:37]all contact with Russia after the invasion of Ukraine.

[192:41]Secretary of State Marco Rubio, National Security Advisor Mike Walts and Middle East envoy Steve

[192:47]Whitcoff met with a Russian delegation led by Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov on Tuesday.

[192:53]During a press conference after the meeting, Rubio said the parties agreed on four points.

[192:59]He said, quote, first is that we are going to work

[193:02]We're going to point our teams respectively to work very quickly to reestablish the functionality

[194:00]since April 2022, Trump's effort to engage with Russia has been condemned by much of

[194:08]Ukrainian President Vladimir Zelensky has said he will refuse to abide by any agreement

[194:16]Which you just love to see. You love to see that he thinks that he's going to stand in

[194:23]the way. But this is a very positive development. And this is consistent with what the Trump

[194:29]administration really is, which is that they are to the core China Hawks. More than anything,

[194:43]What they all have in common, Mike Walts, Elbridge Colby, Rubio, they're all China hawks.

[194:52]And what's the significance of this?

[194:55]When the United States isolated Russia, Russia was forced to deepen its strategic alliance

[195:02]with China.

[195:10]I believe that was two years ago or one year ago. And I've said before on the show,

[195:16]Russia and China are not natural allies. Never have been. As a matter of fact,

[195:21]they're actually natural adversaries. Strictly speaking, because they share such a massive

[195:25]border. They have a massive border. They're both nuclear powers with large militaries.

[195:34]They're both exporters of natural resources. And so by those facts alone, that makes them

[195:40]competitors. It makes them adversaries. And historically, they have been. Even during

[195:47]the communist days, United States engaged in a trilateral diplomacy between Soviet Union

[195:53]and the People's Republic of China. And so the big geopolitical transition that is taking

[196:04]It's a nation of a billion people with a purchasing power

[196:08]that is greater than the United States.

[196:10]Industrial capacity that has already exceeded

[196:13]the United States.

[196:14]They have a large middle class, the people are educated.

[196:18]They have a shipbuilding capacity

[196:20]that rivals the United States.
